messages.content: You are a Lean 4 autoformalization agent. Generate a small ordered sequence of Lean declarations for the provided LaTeX theorem-like unit. Use only the source text, selector plan, previous-project context if shown, and Lean-checked Mathlib signatures in the prompt. The original aligned Lean declarations, names, statements, and proofs are withheld. Return exactly one JSON object.

{
  "benchmark_policy": {
    "gold_comparison_is_posthoc_only": true,
    "posthoc_alignment_hidden": true,
    "target_lean_available_to_generator": false
  },
  "instructions": [
    "Do not use sorry, admit, placeholders, or comments standing in for proof.",
    "Do not include import statements or markdown fences.",
    "Do not ask for or infer the hidden aligned Lean declaration names/statements/proofs.",
    "Treat selector_unchecked_statement_sketch as non-authoritative mathematical intent only; do not copy its Lean syntax verbatim.",
    "Follow the Lean-checked signatures exactly when they differ from the selector's expected shape.",
    "Use the actual Lean-checked argument order from hydrated_mathlib_context. If the selector expected shape conflicts with the checked signature, the checked signature wins.",
    "Never use a hydrated Mathlib exact_identifier whose lean_check.status is not `checked`; treat it as unavailable even if the selector expected it to exist.",
    "Treat fallback_mathlib_candidates as search hints only. Do not cite or use a fallback candidate as a theorem unless its statement in the prompt is sufficient for the proof.",
    "If a selected Mathlib API is field-specific, unit-specific, or otherwise not strong enough for the source statement, say so in notes and generate only declarations justified by the visible context.",
    "Do not invent project helper names that are not shown in available_prior_project_context, needed_project_context, or source context.",
    "When available_prior_project_context contains Lean snippets for project definitions or predicates, use those exact names and statement shapes instead of rephrasing the source with raw hypotheses.",
    "When local_file_predecessor_declarations contains same-file helper declarations, you may reuse those exact helper names and statement shapes; do not infer any hidden target declaration from their file position.",
    "Prefer a narrow declaration sequence over a broad bundled conjunction when the source unit decomposes into multiple Lean declarations.",
    "Keep theorem-local assumptions explicit rather than relying on unavailable global variables.",
    "If local_file_context_candidates show useful namespace, open, notation, or variable commands, you may include the needed commands in lean_file_body before the declarations.",
    "If you use a variable command instead of explicit binders, the command itself must appear in lean_file_body. Do not depend on file-scope variables that are not emitted in your output.",
    "Every identifier used in a theorem statement must be introduced by an explicit binder, local declaration, or imported/opened declaration. In particular, source parameters such as coefficient types, matrix sizes, rings, variables, and typeclass assumptions must be bound in the generated theorem, e.g. `{K : Type*} [CommRing K] {n : ℕ}` when the source quantifies over a commutative ring and a natural number.",
    "If you cannot produce a complete proof from visible context, set status to cannot_prove_from_visible_context and leave lean_file_body empty. Never output Lean containing sorry/admit/placeholders."
  ],
  "lean_environment": {
    "default_opens_used_for_context_hydration": [
      "open scoped Polynomial BigOperators",
      "open PowerSeries Finset"
    ],
    "imports_available_in_verification_file": [
      "Mathlib"
    ],
    "toolchain": "leanprover/lean4:v4.28.0"
  },
  "required_json_schema": {
    "units": [
      {
        "declaration_names": [
          "names introduced in lean_file_body; empty list when status is cannot_prove_from_visible_context"
        ],
        "lean_file_body": "if status is generated: complete Lean declarations only, no imports or markdown; if status is cannot_prove_from_visible_context: exactly empty string",
        "notes": [
          "brief caveats or remaining uncertainty"
        ],
        "status": "generated|cannot_prove_from_visible_context",
        "unit_key": "unit-001",
        "used_context": [
          "source/project/Mathlib facts actually used"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "task": "Produce Lean declarations for each planned declaration task. The output should be a small Lean file body, without imports, containing the ordered declarations needed for this one source unit.",
  "units": [
    {

      ],

      "planned_declarations": [
        {
          "available_prior_project_context": [
            {
              "project_declarations": [
                {
                  "context_source": "aligned_prior_declaration",
                  "declared_source_labels": [
                    "prop.lgv.jordan-2"
                  ],
                  "imports": [
                    "Mathlib",
                    "AlgebraicCombinatorics.Determinants.LGV2"
                  ],
                  "kind": "theorem",
                  "lean_snippet": "/-- Baby Jordan curve theorem: Under NW conditions, paths must intersect.\n    (Proposition prop.lgv.jordan-2)\n    Label: prop.lgv.jordan-2\n\n    **Proof strategy** (from tex source, Section sec.details.det.comb):\n\n    The proof is by strong induction on ℓ(p) + ℓ(p'), the sum of path lengths.\n\n    **Base case**: If ℓ(p) + ℓ(p') = 0, then both paths are empty, so A = B' and A' = B.\n    From the NW conditions:\n    - A'.x ≤ A.x = B'.x ≤ B.x = A'.x, so A'.x = A.x\n    - A'.y ≥ A.y = B'.y ≥ B.y = A'.y, so A'.y = A.y\n    Thus A = A', and A is a common vertex.\n\n    **Induction step**: Assume the theorem holds for smaller path lengths.\n    If A = A', then A is a common vertex and we're done.\n    Otherwise, since A' is weakly NW of A and A ≠ A', we have either:\n    - Case 1: A'.y > A.y (A' strictly north of A)\n    - Case 2: A'.x < A.x (A' strictly west of A)\n\n    In Case 1 (A'.y > A.y):\n    - Let P be the next vertex after A on path p\n    - If the first step of p is north, then P = (A.x, A.y + 1)\n    - Since A'.y > A.y and integers, A'.y ≥ A.y + 1 = P.y\n    - So A' is still weakly NW of P\n    - The rest of p goes from P to B', and p' goes from A' to B\n    - By IH (with smaller path length), these paths intersect\n    - Any common vertex of (rest of p) and p' is also on p\n\n    - If the first step of p is east, then P = (A.x + 1, A.y)\n    - We need to look at the first step of p' and apply IH appropriately\n\n    Case 2 (A'.x < A.x) is symmetric, looking at the first step of p' instead.\n\n    The key insight is that the \"river\" created by path p from A to B' must be\n    crossed by path p' from A' to B, since A' is northwest of A and B' is\n    northwest of B.\n\n    Reference: https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/2870640/ -/\ntheorem baby_jordan_curve (A A' B B' : LatticePoint)",
                  "line_range": [
                    2699,
                    2863
                  ],
                  "module": "AlgebraicCombinatorics.Determinants.LGV1",
                  "name": "LGV1.baby_jordan_curve",
                  "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/Determinants/LGV1.lean",
                  "referenced_source_labels": [
                    "sec.details.det.comb"
                  ],
                  "snippet_policy": "statement_or_definition_body_only",
                  "snippet_truncated": true
                },
                {
                  "context_source": "aligned_prior_declaration",
                  "declared_source_labels": [
                    "prop.lgv.jordan-2"
                  ],
                  "imports": [
                    "Mathlib",
                    "AlgebraicCombinatorics.Determinants.LGV2"
                  ],
                  "kind": "theorem",
                  "lean_snippet": "/-- Under NW conditions, there are no nipats from (A,A') to (B',B).\n    Label: prop.lgv.jordan-2 -/\ntheorem no_nipats_under_nw (A A' B B' : LatticePoint)\n    (hA : isWeaklyNorthwestOf A' A) (hB : isWeaklyNorthwestOf B' B) :\n    numNipats2 A A' B' B = 0",
                  "line_range": [
                    2865,
                    2901
                  ],
                  "module": "AlgebraicCombinatorics.Determinants.LGV1",
                  "name": "LGV1.no_nipats_under_nw",
                  "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/Determinants/LGV1.lean",
                  "referenced_source_labels": [],
                  "snippet_policy": "statement_or_definition_body_only",
                  "snippet_truncated": false
                },
                {
                  "context_source": "aligned_prior_declaration",
                  "declared_source_labels": [
                    "prop.lgv.jordan-2"
                  ],
                  "imports": [
                    "Mathlib"
                  ],
                  "kind": "theorem",
                  "lean_snippet": "/-- Baby Jordan curve theorem (Proposition prop.lgv.jordan-2)\n    Label: prop.lgv.jordan-2\n\n    If A' is weakly northwest of A, and B' is weakly northwest of B,\n    then any path from A to B' and any path from A' to B must intersect.\n\n    This is used to show that non-identity permutations have no nipats.\n\n    **Proof sketch:**\n    Both paths visit vertices with consecutive sum values (x + y).\n    The sum ranges [A.1+A.2, B'.1+B'.2] for p and [A'.1+A'.2, B.1+B.2] for p' overlap.\n    At each sum s in the overlap, path p has x-coordinate x_p(s) and p' has x_p'(s).\n    At s_lo = max(A.1+A.2, A'.1+A'.2): x_p ≥ x_p' (because A.1 ≥ A'.1 and A.2 ≤ A'.2).\n    At s_hi = min(B'.1+B'.2, B.1+B.2): x_p ≤ x_p' (because B'.1 ≤ B.1 and B'.2 ≥ B.2).\n    By discrete IVT, there exists s where x_p(s) = x_p'(s), so the vertices coincide. -/\ntheorem baby_jordan {A A' B B' : ℤ × ℤ}\n    (hxA : xCoord A' ≤ xCoord A) (hyA : yCoord A ≤ yCoord A')\n    (hxB : xCoord B' ≤ xCoord B) (hyB : yCoord B ≤ yCoord B')\n    (p : SimpleDigraph.Path integerLattice) (hp_start : p.start = A) (hp_finish : p.finish = B')\n    (p' : SimpleDigraph.Path integerLattice) (hp'_start : p'.start = A') (hp'_finish : p'.finish = B) :\n    pathsIntersect p p'",
                  "line_range": [
                    3903,
                    4175
                  ],
                  "module": "AlgebraicCombinatorics.Determinants.LGV2",
                  "name": "LGV.baby_jordan",
                  "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/Determinants/LGV2.lean",
                  "referenced_source_labels": [],
                  "snippet_policy": "statement_or_definition_body_only",
                  "snippet_truncated": true
                }
              ],

                "eq.prop.lgv.jordan-2.2"

              "lean_snippet": "lemma mem_vertices_cons (s : LatticeStep) (rest : LatticePath) (start : LatticePoint)\n    (v : LatticePoint) :\n    v ∈ LatticePath.vertices (s :: rest) start ↔\n    v = start ∨ v ∈ LatticePath.vertices rest (s.apply start)",

            "source_text": "\\begin{example}\n\\label{exa.lgv.2paths.2}Let $A=\\left(  0,0\\right)  $ and $A^{\\prime}=\\left(\n-1,1\\right)  $ and $B=\\left(  2,2\\right)  $ and $B^{\\prime}=\\left(\n0,3\\right)  $. Then, the claim of Proposition \\ref{prop.lgv.2paths.count}\nsimplifies, since $\\left(  \\text{\\# of nipats from }\\left(  A,A^{\\prime\n}\\right)  \\text{ to }\\left(  B^{\\prime},B\\right)  \\right)  =0$ in this case.\nHere is a picture of the four points that should make this visually clear:\n\\[%\n%TCIMACRO{\\TeXButton{just four points}{% [tikz figure removed]}}%\n%BeginExpansion\n% [tikz figure removed]%\n%EndExpansion\n\\ \\ .\n\\]\n\n\\end{example}"

            "id": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/Determinants/LGV1.tex:prop.lgv.jordan-2",

            "parse_warnings": [],

            "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/Determinants/LGV1.tex",
            "referenced_labels": [],
            "source_text": "\\begin{proposition}\n[baby Jordan curve theorem]\\label{prop.lgv.jordan-2}Let $A$, $B$, $A^{\\prime}$\nand $B^{\\prime}$ be four lattice points satisfying%\n\\begin{align}\n\\operatorname*{x}\\left(  A^{\\prime}\\right)   &  \\leq\\operatorname*{x}\\left(\nA\\right)  ,\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\operatorname*{y}\\left(  A^{\\prime}\\right)\n\\geq\\operatorname*{y}\\left(  A\\right)  ,\\label{eq.prop.lgv.jordan-2.1}\\\\\n\\operatorname*{x}\\left(  B^{\\prime}\\right)   &  \\leq\\operatorname*{x}\\left(\nB\\right)  ,\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\operatorname*{y}\\left(  B^{\\prime}\\right)\n\\geq\\operatorname*{y}\\left(  B\\right)  . \\label{eq.prop.lgv.jordan-2.2}%\n\\end{align}\nHere, $\\operatorname*{x}\\left(  P\\right)  $ and $\\operatorname*{y}\\left(\nP\\right)  $ denote the two coordinates of any point $P\\in\\mathbb{Z}^{2}$.\n\nLet $p$ be any path from $A$ to $B^{\\prime}$. Let $p^{\\prime}$ be any path\nfrom $A^{\\prime}$ to $B$. Then, $p$ and $p^{\\prime}$ have a vertex in common.\n\\end{proposition}"
          }
        }

        "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/Determinants/LGV1.tex",
        "referenced_labels": [],
        "source_text": "\\begin{corollary}\n\\label{cor.lgv.binom-unimod}Let $n,k\\in\\mathbb{N}$. Then, $\\dbinom{n}{k}%\n^{2}\\geq\\dbinom{n}{k-1}\\cdot\\dbinom{n}{k+1}$.\n\\end{corollary}"
